This generic format is a prerequisite for option processing; however, the semantic scope of an option (whether it applies to the whole client, a specific Identity Association (IA), or addresses within an IA) is determined by its `option-code` and context, as detailed in subsequent sections on option encapsulation.

During the processing of a DHCPv6 response message traveling from a server back towards a client, a relay agent that had previously inserted one or more options (such as an Interface-Id) into the forwarded request and requested the server to echo them back, receives those echoed options in the corresponding response. The relay agent MUST use the information contained within these echoed options (e.g., to identify the correct client-facing interface) to correctly forward the response packet back to the originating client. Alternatively, the relay agent MAY use these echoed options for other internal needs, such as logging or policy enforcement. This rule applies specifically when a stateless relay agent is removing an encapsulation level from a server-bound response and requires information that was not retained locally. The expected outcome is the successful delivery of the response to the intended client or the fulfillment of the relay's ancillary functions. A prerequisite for this rule is that the relay agent must have initially included the relevant options in its relay-forward message and requested the server to echo them back in the relay-reply message.

In the DHCPv6 protocol, the option-code is a 2-octet (16-bit) unsigned integer field that uniquely identifies the type of option being carried within an option structure. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any DHCPv6 option, as defined in the general option format. The expected outcome is that the option-code is encoded as exactly two octets in network byte order, enabling the recipient to correctly interpret the option's purpose and subsequently process the associated variable-length option-data based on the specified option-len. This rule is a prerequisite for all option handling and must be followed regardless of the option's scope (client, IA, or address-specific).

The option-code is an unsigned 2-octet integer that uniquely identifies the specific option type carried within a DHCPv6 option. This rule governs the encoding of the option header, which must be present in every DHCPv6 option as defined in the general format. It applies during the construction or parsing of any DHCPv6 message, as each option begins with this mandatory field. The expected outcome is that the option-code correctly specifies the option's purpose (e.g., 23 for DNS Recursive Name Server), enabling the recipient to interpret the subsequent option-len and option-data fields accordingly. This rule is a prerequisite for all option processing; the option-code must be defined in the specification (e.g., for client, IA, or address-specific options) and must be accurately set to ensure proper scoping and handling of the encapsulated data.

In the DHCPv6 protocol, the option-len field is a 2-octet unsigned integer that specifies the length, in octets, of the variable-length option-data field within a DHCP option. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any DHCPv6 option, as defined in the option format (Figure 12), and must be accurately set to reflect the exact size of the option-data to ensure proper encapsulation and scope handling. The expected outcome is that receivers can correctly interpret the option boundaries, allowing the protocol to support options scoped to the client, Identity Associations (IAs), or specific addresses. Prerequisites include a valid option-code identifying the option type, and the option-len must be consistent with the actual data length to avoid parsing errors or malformed packets.

The option-data field within a DHCPv6 option is a variable-length field that carries the specific data for the option, as defined by its option-code. The exact length of this field in octets is explicitly provided by the preceding option-len field. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any DHCPv6 option during message formation or interpretation. The expected outcome is that the length of the option-data field must precisely match the value specified in option-len, ensuring correct message parsing and preventing buffer over-read or under-read errors. A prerequisite for applying this rule is that the option-len field has been correctly read or set according to the specific option's definition, which dictates the format and permissible content of the option-data.

Within the DHCPv6 protocol, specifically in the construction of DHCP options as defined in Section 21.1, the `option-len` field is a fixed, 2-octet (16-bit) unsigned integer field. This rule governs the format of all DHCPv6 options and applies whenever a DHCPv6 message includes an options field, which is a common and fundamental part of the protocol exchange. The `option-len` field must immediately follow the 2-octet `option-code` field. Its purpose is to specify the exact length, in octets, of the subsequent variable-length `option-data` field for that specific option. The expected outcome is that a compliant implementation will correctly parse this 2-octet value to determine how many octets to read as the option's data, ensuring accurate interpretation of the option's contents. This rule is a prerequisite for correctly processing any DHCPv6 option, as the format of the `option-data` itself is dependent on the option type defined by the `option-code`.

DHCP options are scoped by using encapsulation, meaning that certain options are nested within other option structures to define their applicability. This rule governs how options are organized to specify whether they apply to the entire client, to a specific Identity Association (IA), or to individual addresses within an IA. It applies during the construction and interpretation of DHCPv6 messages, particularly when options need to be associated with specific entities like IAs or addresses. The expected outcome is that options are correctly encapsulated within the appropriate parent options (e.g., IA_NA or IA_TA options for IA-specific options, or IA Address options for address-specific options), ensuring proper scope and preventing misinterpretation. Prerequisites include the presence of IA or address structures in the message, and the option definitions must support encapsulation as specified in sections 21.4, 21.5, and 21.6 of the protocol.

The option-len field in the Client Identifier option specifies the length, in octets, of the following DUID (DHCP Unique Identifier) that identifies the client. This rule applies when constructing or parsing a DHCPv6 Client Identifier option, ensuring the length field accurately reflects the variable-length DUID's size. The expected outcome is correct message interpretation, where the recipient can precisely locate and extract the DUID based on this length. Prerequisites include that a valid DUID (as defined in Section 11) must be present, and its length must not exceed 65535 octets, as constrained by the 16-bit option-len field.

The Server Identifier option (OPTION_SERVERID, code 2) is used in DHCPv6 messages to carry a DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID) that uniquely identifies the server. This rule governs the inclusion of server identity in messages where a server needs to be explicitly identified, such as in Advertise, Reply, Reconfigure, and other server-originated messages. The expected outcome is that the client can reliably identify and authenticate the specific server it is communicating with. The option must contain a valid DUID as defined in Section 11, and the option-len field must accurately reflect the length of that DUID in octets. This option is mandatory in server responses when a server identifier is required by the protocol exchange.
res_type: Advertise, Reply, Reconfigure, and other server-originated messages

rule: DHCPv6 options are organized into a three-tier scoping hierarchy that determines their applicability and encapsulation. Options that apply generally to the entire client (e.g., DNS server list) are placed directly in the main message. Options specific to an Identity Association (IA), such as IA-specific timers, must be encapsulated within the corresponding IA option (IA_NA, IA_TA, or IA_PD). Options specific to individual addresses or prefixes within an IA (e.g., preferred/valid lifetimes for a specific address) must be further encapsulated within the IA Address (IAADDR) or IA Prefix (IAPREFIX) sub-option of that IA. This rule applies during the construction and parsing of any DHCPv6 message (Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply, etc.). The expected outcome is correct option placement, ensuring that options are interpreted in the proper context by both clients and servers. A prerequisite is that the message contains at least one IA option if IA-specific or address-specific options are to be included.

The Server Identifier option (OPTION_SERVERID, code 2) is used by a DHCPv6 server to uniquely identify itself in messages where server identification is required, such as in Advertise, Reply, Reconfigure, and Information-Reply messages. This rule applies during the DHCPv6 message exchange when a server needs to assert its identity to a client, allowing the client to distinguish between multiple servers. The expected outcome is the inclusion of this option containing the server's DUID (as defined in Section 11) with the correct option-len specifying the DUID's length in octets. A prerequisite is that the server must have a generated or configured DUID prior to sending any message requiring this option.

The Client Identifier option (OPTION_CLIENTID, code 1) is used by a DHCPv6 client to include its unique DUID (as defined in Section 11) in messages sent to a server, enabling the server to reliably identify and associate the client with its configuration state (e.g., leases, parameters). This option is mandatory in all client-initiated messages (e.g., Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, Release, Decline) and must be constructed with the option-len field accurately reflecting the variable-length DUID. The server uses this identifier to process requests and manage client-specific resources; a missing or malformed Client Identifier option in a client message is an error condition that may cause the server to discard the message.
req_type: Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, Release, Decline

rule: The option-len field in the Client Identifier option specifies the length, in octets, of the following DUID (DHCP Unique Identifier) that identifies the client. This rule applies when constructing or parsing a DHCPv6 Client Identifier option, ensuring the length field accurately reflects the variable-length DUID's size. The expected outcome is correct message interpretation, where the recipient can precisely locate and extract the DUID based on this length. Prerequisites include that a valid DUID (as defined in Section 11) must be present, and its length must not exceed 65535 octets, as constrained by the 16-bit option-len field.

The Server Identifier option is used by DHCPv6 servers to uniquely identify themselves in messages such as Advertise, Reply, Reconfigure, and Information-Request responses, enabling clients to distinguish between multiple servers. This rule applies when a server needs to include its identifier in a DHCPv6 message, and it specifies that the option must follow the format depicted in Figure 14, which consists of the fixed fields option-code (set to OPTION_SERVERID, value 2) and option-len (indicating the length in octets of the DUID), followed by the variable-length DUID (as defined in Section 11) that constitutes the server's unique identifier. The expected outcome is a properly structured option that allows clients to reliably identify the server, with the prerequisite that a valid DUID has been generated and assigned to the server prior to option construction.

The Client Identifier option is used to carry a DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID) that uniquely identifies the client to the server, as defined in Section 11 of the DHCPv6 specification. This option applies when a client sends messages to a server, such as in Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind messages, to allow the server to associate the request with a specific client identity. The expected outcome is that the server can correctly identify the client and manage lease assignments or configuration parameters accordingly. The prerequisite is that the client must have a valid DUID generated according to the DUID generation rules. The format of the option is as follows: it begins with a 16-bit OPTION_CLIENTID field set to 1, followed by a 16-bit option-len field specifying the length of the DUID in octets, and then the variable-length DUID itself. This structure ensures that the client identifier is properly encapsulated within DHCPv6 messages for reliable identification.

The Client Identifier option (OPTION_CLIENTID, code 1) is used by a DHCPv6 client to uniquely identify itself to a server. This rule governs the format of this mandatory option, which must be included in all client-originated DHCPv6 messages (e.g., Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind) except for Information-request messages. The option carries a DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID) as defined in Section 11 of the specification. The expected format is a fixed header containing the 2-byte option code (1) and a 2-byte length field specifying the DUID's size in octets, followed by the variable-length DUID itself. The server uses this identifier to associate the client with leases and configuration data. A valid DUID must be constructed according to the rules in Section 11 prior to using this option.

The Preference option, used by a DHCPv6 server in an Advertise message to influence client server selection, must have an option length field of exactly 1 octet. This fixed length corresponds to and defines the size of the subsequent preference value field, which is a single unsigned integer. This rule applies when constructing or parsing the option, and adherence ensures correct interpretation of the server's preference level.

The Preference option is sent by a server to a client within an Advertise message to influence the client's selection of a server during the initial server discovery phase. This rule applies when a server responds to a client's Solicit message and wishes to indicate its relative desirability compared to other servers. The option contains a 1-octet unsigned integer preference value, where a higher value signifies a higher preference level. The expected outcome is that the client, when receiving multiple Advertise messages, will preferentially select the server with the highest preference value, as detailed in Section 18.2.9. A prerequisite is that the server must be configured to send Advertise messages and must include the OPTION_PREFERENCE (code 7) with a valid length field.

During the DHCPv6 server discovery phase, when a server sends an Advertise message in response to a client's Solicit, it MAY include a Preference option (OPTION_PREFERENCE, code 7) to influence the client's server selection process. This option contains a 1-octet unsigned integer preference value indicating the server's relative desirability. The rule applies specifically to the construction of the Advertise message, and its expected outcome is to provide the client with a hint for choosing among multiple responding servers, as detailed in Section 18.2.9 for client-side interpretation. The inclusion of this option is optional and serves as a control mechanism only when multiple servers are available for selection.

The Preference option is used by a DHCPv6 server to influence a client's server selection process during the address/configuration exchange. This rule applies when a server sends an Advertise message in response to a client's Solicit message, and it governs the specific format of the Preference option within that message. The expected outcome is that the option is correctly structured with a fixed option code (OPTION_PREFERENCE, value 7), a 1-octet length field (always set to 1), and a 1-octet unsigned integer preference value field indicating the server's relative desirability. A prerequisite for this rule is that the server is configured to express a preference and includes this optional option in its Advertise message. The client is then expected to use this value, as defined in Section 18.2.9, to prioritize server responses.

The Preference option, sent by a server in an Advertise message, contains a 1-octet unsigned integer value that indicates the server's relative preference level. This rule governs how a DHCPv6 client must interpret and use this value during server selection. Specifically, when a client receives multiple Advertise messages, it should use the preference value as a primary criterion to choose which server to send a Request to, with higher values indicating greater preference. The rule applies during the initial address/configuration exchange when the client is in the Solicit-Advertise-Request-Reply sequence. For detailed specifications on the client's selection algorithm, handling of equal or absent preference values, and the exact interpretation of the data value (including its range and default), refer to Section 18.2.9. The prerequisite is that the client must have sent a Solicit message and received one or more Advertise messages containing this optional option.

The Elapsed Time Option, identified by option-code 8 (OPTION_ELAPSED_TIME), must have its option-len field set to 2. This fixed length indicates that the subsequent elapsed-time field is exactly 2 octets (16 bits), representing an unsigned integer that measures the time in hundredths of a second since the client initiated the current DHCP transaction. This rule applies whenever a client includes the Elapsed Time Option in a DHCPv6 message, as mandated for indicating the duration of the message exchange. The expected outcome is a properly formatted option where the option-len consistently specifies a 2-octet data field, enabling servers and relay agents to accurately interpret the elapsed time for policy decisions, such as failover handling. The prerequisite is that the client has begun a DHCP transaction, with the elapsed-time set to 0 in the first message and incremented thereafter, using 0xffff for values exceeding the representable maximum.

During any DHCPv6 message exchange, a client MUST include the Elapsed Time option (OPTION_ELAPSED_TIME, code 8) in all transmitted messages to inform servers and relay agents of the duration, in hundredths of a second, that the client has been attempting to complete the current transaction. This rule applies from the initiation of a DHCP transaction, where the first message must set the elapsed-time field to 0, and continues for all subsequent messages in that exchange. The expected outcome is that servers and relay agents receive accurate timing data, which they use as policy input—for instance, enabling a secondary server to respond if a primary server is unreachable after a reasonable period. The client must measure time from the transmission of the first message in the exchange and use the value 0xffff to indicate any elapsed time exceeding the maximum representable value (approximately 655.35 seconds).

During the DHCPv6 message exchange, the client MUST include the Elapsed Time option (OPTION_ELAPSED_TIME, code 8) to indicate the time elapsed since it initiated the current transaction, measured in hundredths of a second. The elapsed-time field is a 16-bit unsigned integer, allowing a maximum representable value of 655.35 seconds (0xfffe). If the actual elapsed time exceeds this maximum, the client MUST set the elapsed-time field to 0xffff to signal an overflow condition. This rule applies to all client messages after the initial message in an exchange (where the elapsed-time is set to 0). Servers and relay agents use this value for policy decisions, such as enabling a secondary server to respond when a primary server is unreachable.
req_type: client messages after the initial message

rule: The Elapsed Time option (OPTION_ELAPSED_TIME, code 8) is a mandatory client-included option that carries a 2-octet value representing the time, in hundredths of a second, since the client initiated the current DHCPv6 transaction (set to 0 in the first message). This rule governs how servers and relay agents use this elapsed-time value as a policy input for managing server responses. Specifically, it applies during the message exchange when a client is awaiting a reply, allowing a secondary DHCP server to intervene and respond to the client's request if the elapsed time indicates that a primary server has failed to reply within a configured, reasonable threshold. The expected outcome is improved fault tolerance and responsiveness, ensuring the client receives a timely DHCP service even if the primary server is unresponsive. A prerequisite is that the network must be configured with multiple DHCP servers employing a policy that evaluates this option, and the client must have included the option correctly, using 0xffff to represent any overflow beyond the maximum representable time.

The Elapsed Time Option is a mandatory option that a DHCPv6 client MUST include in its messages to inform servers and relay agents of the duration of its current transaction attempt. This option applies during any client-initiated message exchange (e.g., Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind) starting from the first message in that sequence. The option format consists of a fixed structure: an option-code for OPTION_ELAPSED_TIME (8), an option-len field (which MUST be 2), and a 2-octet elapsed-time field containing an unsigned integer. The elapsed-time value represents the time, in hundredths of a second, since the client sent the first message of the current exchange; for that initial message, the client MUST set this field to 0. If the elapsed time exceeds the maximum representable value (0xffff, or approximately 655.35 seconds), the client MUST use the value 0xffff. The expected outcome is that servers and relay agents can use this elapsed time for policy decisions, such as enabling a secondary server to respond if a primary server is unresponsive beyond a configured threshold.

The Relay Message option, used within DHCPv6 Relay-forward or Relay-reply messages to encapsulate and transport a complete DHCP message, must be formatted with the OPTION_RELAY_MSG code (value 9) and an option-len field. This rule applies when a relay agent is forwarding a client message toward a server or when a server is returning a response through relay agents. The option-len field must accurately specify the length in octets of the DHCP-relay-message field that follows, which contains the verbatim relayed DHCP message. The correct formatting ensures that relay agents and servers can properly parse, forward, or extract the encapsulated message for further processing in the relay chain.

In the DHCPv6 protocol, when a relay agent encapsulates a DHCP message within a Relay-forward or Relay-reply message using the Relay Message option (OPTION_RELAY_MSG, code 9), the option-len field must accurately specify the length in octets of the DHCP-relay-message field. This rule applies during the construction of relayed messages, ensuring that receiving entities can correctly parse the encapsulated DHCP message. The DHCP-relay-message field contains the original DHCP message being relayed verbatim, and its length must be precisely defined to maintain protocol integrity and avoid parsing errors.

The Relay Message Option (OPTION_RELAY_MSG, code 9) is used to encapsulate a complete DHCP message within a Relay-forward or Relay-reply message. This option applies when a relay agent forwards client-originated messages to servers (Relay-forward) or when servers return responses back through relay agents to clients (Relay-reply). The option consists of the standard option-code and option-len fields, followed by the DHCP-relay-message field containing the encapsulated DHCP message. The option-len field must accurately specify the length in octets of the DHCP-relay-message field. When followed correctly, this enables relay agents to transparently transport DHCP messages between clients and servers without modifying the original message content.

The Relay Message option (OPTION_RELAY_MSG, code 9) is used to encapsulate a complete DHCP message within either a Relay-forward or Relay-reply message during DHCPv6 relay agent operations. This rule applies when a relay agent forwards client-originated messages toward the server (Relay-forward) or when the server's response is being relayed back toward the client (Relay-reply). The option must include the standard option code and option-len fields, with the DHCP-relay-message field containing the verbatim relayed message. In a Relay-forward, this is the original client message; in a Relay-reply, it is the server's response destined for the client identified by the peer-address field. The expected outcome is the correct encapsulation and transport of DHCP messages through relay agents, enabling communication across network segments. The prerequisite is that a relay agent is involved in the message exchange, and the message being carried must be a valid DHCP message.

The Interface-Id option's interface-id field contains an opaque, variable-length identifier generated by a relay agent. The exact length of this interface-id field in octets is explicitly defined by the value in the option-len field of the option header. This length specification is mandatory for parsing the option correctly in Relay-forward and Relay-reply messages, ensuring the relay agent can accurately identify the intended interface when relaying the DHCPv6 message to the client. The server must treat this value as an opaque byte sequence without internal parsing, using it solely for policy matching based on its exact length and content.

The Server Unicast option, identified by option code OPTION_UNICAST (12), is used by a DHCPv6 server to authorize a client to send subsequent messages directly to the server via unicast, bypassing relay agents. This rule applies when the server includes this option in a response to the client, specifying the exact 128-bit server address in the server-address field. The expected outcome is that the client, upon receiving this option, will use the provided address for unicast communication where permitted, thereby avoiding relay agent involvement. A critical prerequisite is that relay agents must not be sending relay agent options, as their omission would otherwise be necessary for proper message handling; the server must reject any inappropriately unicast messages to enforce relay use when agent options are required.

The Relay Message option (OPTION_RELAY_MSG, code 9) is used to encapsulate a complete DHCP message within a Relay-forward or Relay-reply message. This rule governs the handling of the encapsulated DHCP message (the DHCP-relay-message field) based on the type of the containing relay message. In a Relay-forward message, which is sent by a relay agent toward the server, the DHCP-relay-message MUST contain the original client message or a previously relayed message, forwarded without modification to the next relay agent or server. In a Relay-reply message, which is sent by a server back toward the client, the DHCP-relay-message contains the server's response that MUST be extracted and relayed to the specific relay agent or client identified by the peer-address field in the header of the Relay-reply message. The option-len field specifies the exact length in octets of the encapsulated DHCP message. This mechanism is fundamental for multi-hop DHCP communication, ensuring end-to-end message integrity across relay agents.

A DHCP server must reject any client messages that are inappropriately sent via unicast (i.e., directly to the server's address) when relay agent options are required for proper server processing. This rule applies after the server has not provided the client with a valid Server Unicast option, which would explicitly permit direct unicast communication. The expected outcome is that such rejected unicast messages will force the client to revert to using the standard relayed message path, ensuring that all necessary relay agent options are included. The prerequisite for this enforcement is that the server is configured to require or utilize options that can only be provided by relay agents (e.g., for network topology information or policy enforcement).

The Server Unicast option, identified by OPTION_UNICAST (code 12), must have a fixed length of 16 octets. This length corresponds to the 128-bit IPv6 address contained in the server-address field, which specifies the unicast address the client should use for direct communication. This rule applies when a DHCPv6 server includes this option in a response to inform the client that it may bypass relay agents and send subsequent messages directly via unicast. The server should only send this option when relay agents are not providing relay agent options, as its use prevents messages from being relayed. If a client receives this option, it may—where permissible—send messages directly to the specified address, and the server must reject any inappropriately unicast messages to ensure proper relay behavior when needed.

When a DHCPv6 server sends a Server Unicast option (OPTION_UNICAST) to a client, it authorizes the client to send subsequent messages directly to the server's specified unicast address. This rule applies after the client receives the option and chooses to use unicast for permissible message types (e.g., Renew, Rebind, Release, Decline). As a result, those unicast messages bypass relay agents, meaning they will not be forwarded by relays and will not contain any relay agent options that would otherwise be added by relays. The expected outcome is reduced network overhead and direct client-server communication, but this should only be used when relay agent options are not required for server processing, as their absence may affect decision-making. A prerequisite is that the server must validate that no essential relay agent options are in use before sending this option, and the server must reject any unicast messages that are inappropriate or that should have been relayed.

When a DHCPv6 server sends a Server Unicast option (OPTION_UNICAST) to a client, it authorizes the client to send subsequent messages directly to the server's specified unicast address, bypassing relay agents. This rule applies during the server's response phase in a message exchange where the server is considering whether to include the Server Unicast option. The server must only include this option if it determines that no relay agents in the communication path are including relay agent options (e.g., Interface-Id or Remote-Id options) in their forwarded messages. The expected outcome of following this rule is that all client messages requiring relay agent context will continue to be relayed, ensuring necessary relay agent options are present for server processing. A prerequisite is that the server must be able to inspect incoming messages to verify the absence of relay agent options before deciding to send the Server Unicast option.

When constructing an IA Address option for DHCPv6, which is used to specify an IPv6 address within an IA_NA or IA_TA option, the option-len field must be calculated as 24 octets plus the total length of any encapsulated IAaddr-options. This rule applies during the creation of the IA Address option by either the client or server, ensuring the option length accurately reflects the fixed-size fields (IPv6 address, preferred lifetime, and valid lifetime, totaling 24 octets) plus any variable-length options. The expected outcome is a properly formatted option where the length field matches the actual packet structure, enabling correct parsing. This calculation is a prerequisite for the option to be valid, and the IAaddr-options field length is derived by subtracting 24 from the option-len value when processing the option.

The IAaddr-options field, within an IA Address option, is used to encapsulate and carry DHCPv6 options that are specifically associated with the IPv6 address defined in that IA Address option. This rule applies when constructing or parsing an IA Address option, which itself is encapsulated within an IA_NA-options or IA_TA-options field. The expected outcome is that any option pertaining to the configuration or status of this specific address (e.g., a Status Code option indicating the success or failure of an address assignment operation) must be placed within this IAaddr-options field, rather than at a higher level like the IA_NA-options. A prerequisite is the existence of a valid IA Address option containing the IPv6-address, preferred-lifetime, and valid-lifetime fields. The length of the IAaddr-options field is variable and is calculated as the total option-len minus 24 octets.

The IAaddr-options field, within an IA Address option, contains DHCPv6 options that are specifically associated with the IPv6 address defined in that option. This rule applies when an IA Address option is included in a DHCPv6 message, either encapsulated within an IA_NA or IA_TA option. The field is used to convey address-specific configuration parameters or status information, such as a Status Code option to indicate the success or failure of an operation for this particular address. The length of this variable-length field is calculated as the total option length (option-len) minus 24 octets. When present, the client or server must process these encapsulated options in the context of the associated address.

The IAaddr-options field within an IA Address option is a variable-length field that contains sub-options specific to the associated IPv6 address, such as status codes. Its length is derived by subtracting the fixed-length portion of the IA Address option (24 octets, which includes the 16-octet IPv6 address and the two 4-octet lifetime fields) from the total length specified in the option-len field of the IA Address option header. This calculation must be performed whenever an IA Address option is parsed or constructed to correctly handle the encapsulated options. The rule applies during both message encoding by a server and decoding by a client, ensuring proper interpretation of the option's variable-length data.

The IA Address option, which specifies an IPv6 address and its associated lifetimes for an Identity Association (IA), must be properly encapsulated within the appropriate options field of its parent IA option. Specifically, when the address is associated with an IA for Non-temporary Addresses (IA_NA), the IA Address option must be placed within the IA_NA-options field of that IA_NA option. Conversely, when the address is associated with an IA for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA), it must be placed within the IA_TA-options field of that IA_TA option. This rule applies during the construction of DHCPv6 messages (such as Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply, Renew, Rebind) when a server is assigning addresses or a client is requesting/renewing them. The expected outcome is a correctly structured DHCPv6 option hierarchy that allows the protocol to unambiguously associate addresses with their respective IAs and manage their lifetimes. A prerequisite is that a valid IA_NA or IA_TA option must already be present in the message to serve as the encapsulating container.

When an IA Address option is included in a DHCPv6 message, the success or failure of operations specifically pertaining to that individual IPv6 address (such as assignment, renewal, or release) must be communicated through a Status Code option placed within the IAaddr-options field of that IA Address option. This rule applies during the protocol exchange when a server is responding to a client's request concerning an address within an IA_NA or IA_TA, or when a client is providing status feedback to a server. The expected outcome is that the recipient (client or server) can determine the precise result for that specific address by examining the Status Code within its encapsulating IAaddr-options. A prerequisite for this rule is that an operation on the address warrants a status indication; if no Status Code option is present, it implies success for that address. The detailed format and semantics of the Status Code option are defined in Section 21.13.

The Rapid Commit option, used to signal the two-message exchange for address assignment, must have an option-len field set to 0, indicating that the option contains no data beyond the option-code. This rule applies when a client includes the option in a Solicit message to indicate readiness for the rapid commit exchange, or when a server includes it in a Reply message to confirm the exchange. The expected outcome is that the option is correctly formatted without additional payload, enabling the streamlined address assignment process. Prerequisites include the client's willingness to use rapid commit and the server's support for this feature, as specified in the protocol's message exchange procedures.

The Rapid Commit option (OPTION_RAPID_COMMIT, code 14) is used to signal and enable the two-message exchange (Solicit/Reply) for rapid address assignment, bypassing the standard four-message exchange (Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply). This rule applies when a client includes the Rapid Commit option in a Solicit message, indicating its readiness to commit to the first server that responds. Upon receiving such a Solicit, a server that supports rapid commit MUST include the Rapid Commit option in its Reply and immediately commit the offered leases to the client without waiting for a confirmation. The expected outcome is a faster address assignment process, but with the risk that multiple responding servers may commit unused leases if more than one server replies, potentially leading to stale DNS records or inaccurate leasequery data. To mitigate this, the DHCP service should be designed to ensure only one server responds, or short lease lifetimes should be used for rapid assignments.

When a DHCPv6 server receives a Solicit message containing the Rapid Commit option from a client and chooses to complete the two-message rapid-commit exchange (as defined in Section 18.2.1), the server MUST include the Rapid Commit option in its Reply message. This inclusion signals to the client that the exchange is using the rapid-commit mechanism, allowing immediate address or prefix assignment without the usual four-message exchange. The rule applies specifically when the server is responding to a Solicit that includes the Rapid Commit option and the server is configured to support rapid commit. The expected outcome is a streamlined assignment process, but note that this mechanism carries a risk of unused committed leases if multiple servers respond, which should be mitigated through service design or short lease lifetimes.

The Rapid Commit option, identified by option-code OPTION_RAPID_COMMIT (14) and a fixed option-len of 0, enables a two-message address assignment exchange (Solicit/Reply) instead of the standard four-message exchange. This rule applies when a client includes the option in a Solicit message to indicate its readiness for rapid commitment, and a server must include it in the Reply to confirm the accelerated process. The expected outcome is a faster lease assignment, but servers must commit leases immediately upon sending the Reply without client confirmation, risking unused leases if multiple servers respond. Prerequisites include client support for rapid commit and server configuration to handle potential lease conflicts, such as using short lease lifetimes or ensuring single-server responses to minimize resource waste.

During the DHCPv6 Rapid Commit two-message exchange (Solicit/Reply), when a server sends a Reply in response to a client's Solicit that contains the Rapid Commit option, the server must immediately commit the address or prefix leases specified in the Reply to its binding database for the client. This commitment occurs even though the server receives no subsequent confirmation (such as a Request or Renew message) that the client successfully received and accepted the Reply. This rule applies specifically to the rapid-commit mechanism defined in Section 18.2.1, which bypasses the standard four-message exchange. A prerequisite is that the client's initial Solicit message must include the Rapid Commit option (OPTION_RAPID_COMMIT). The expected outcome is an accelerated lease assignment, but with the inherent risk that if multiple servers respond, all but one will commit unused leases, potentially leading to stale DNS records and inaccurate leasequery responses.

A client MAY include the Rapid Commit option (option-code 14, option-len 0) in a Solicit message to signal its readiness to use the accelerated two-message address assignment exchange (Solicit/Reply) defined in Section 18.2.1, bypassing the standard four-message exchange. This rule applies when the client initiates the address configuration process and is willing to accept an immediate binding without subsequent confirmation messages. The expected outcome is that a server receiving this option may respond directly with a Reply message containing committed address and configuration leases, thereby reducing latency and network traffic. However, the client must be aware that including this option carries the risk of multiple servers committing unused leases if more than one server responds, which can lead to stale DNS records and inaccurate leasequery data. Therefore, this option should only be used in environments where the DHCP service is designed to ensure single-server responses or where short lease lifetimes mitigate the impact of unused commitments.

The Option Request Option (ORO) is a DHCPv6 option used by a client to explicitly request a list of configuration options from a server. Its format, defined in Figure 18, uses the fixed option-code `OPTION_ORO (6)`. This rule applies when a client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message. The client MUST include this option to inform the server which specific top-level options it wishes to receive in the server's response. The `option-len` field MUST be calculated as two octets per requested option code. The expected outcome is that the server, upon receiving a valid ORO, will include the requested options (if supported and permissible) in its reply. Prerequisites include that the ORO MUST NOT contain certain prohibited options (e.g., Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA) as listed in the specification, and it should generally only contain top-level options, not those encapsulated within other options, unless explicitly defined by an extension.

The Option Request option (OPTION_ORO, code 6) is used by a DHCPv6 client to specify a list of configuration options it requests from the server. This option MUST be included by the client in Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages to inform the server which options the client wishes to receive. The format consists of the OPTION_ORO code, an option-len field (calculated as 2 times the number of requested options), and a sequence of 2-octet requested option codes. Only top-level options that are not explicitly prohibited (such as Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA, and others listed in the specification) MAY be included. The server uses this list to determine which additional options to include in its response; any top-level option not listed in a valid Option Request option will not be sent by the server. Options encapsulated within container options generally SHOULD NOT be listed, unless explicitly defined as an exception (e.g., for feature signaling). Specific message types may require certain option codes to be included as per Table 4.

In the DHCPv6 Option Request option (OPTION_ORO, code 6), which is used by a client in Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages to specify a list of top-level options it requests from the server, the option-len field MUST be calculated as 2 times the number of requested option codes. This is because each requested-option-code is a 2-octet unsigned integer, and the option-len represents the total length in octets of the requested option codes field. The rule applies when constructing or validating the Option Request option, ensuring the length field accurately reflects the number of option codes listed. The expected outcome is a correctly formatted option where the option-len matches the cumulative size of the requested option codes, enabling proper parsing by the server. Prerequisites include that only permissible top-level options (excluding prohibited options such as Client Identifier, Server Identifier, and IA_NA) are included, and each option code is unique within the list.

The Option Request option (OPTION_ORO, code 6) is used by a DHCPv6 client to explicitly identify a list of configuration options it requests from a server. This rule applies when a client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, and it governs how the client informs the server which top-level options should be included in the server's response. The expected outcome is that the server, upon receiving a valid Option Request option, will include the requested options (if supported and permissible) in its reply, thereby enabling the client to obtain the necessary configuration parameters. Prerequisites include: the client MUST include this option in the specified message types; the option-len must be exactly twice the number of requested option codes; and the requested option codes MUST be valid top-level options, excluding a defined set of prohibited options (such as Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA, and others listed in the specification). Additionally, encapsulated options generally SHOULD NOT be requested, though exceptions may be defined for specific features.

Within the DHCPv6 Option Request option (OPTION_ORO), which is used by a client in Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages to specify a list of desired options from the server, each requested option code is encoded as a 2-octet field containing an unsigned integer. This field format directly determines the calculation of the option-len field (which is 2 times the number of requested options) and applies to all top-level options the client wishes to receive, excluding those explicitly prohibited (such as Client Identifier or Server Identifier). The rule ensures proper parsing and generation of the option list, and adherence is required for the server to correctly interpret and fulfill the client's request for optional configuration parameters.

The requested-option-code-n field within the Option Request option (OPTION_ORO) contains the 2-octet option code for a specific configuration parameter or feature that the client is requesting from the server. This rule applies when a client constructs a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message and must include an Option Request option to inform the server of the options it desires in the server's response. The expected outcome is that the server, upon receiving a valid Option Request option, will include the requested options (if supported and permissible) in its reply. Prerequisites include that the requested option must be a top-level option (not an encapsulated sub-option, unless explicitly defined as an exception, such as the Prefix Exclude option) and must not be from the prohibited list of options (e.g., Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA, etc.), as these are either mandatory for the message exchange or handled through other mechanisms. The option-len field must be correctly set to twice the number of requested options.

When a client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it MUST include an Option Request option (OPTION_ORO) to specify which additional configuration options it wishes to receive from the server. For specific message types, certain option codes are mandatory to include in this list, as detailed in Table 4. This requirement ensures that the server can properly interpret the client's request and provide the necessary configuration parameters. The rule applies only after the client has determined its message type and is constructing the Option Request option. The expected outcome is that the server, upon receiving a compliant request, will include the requested mandatory options in its response, facilitating successful configuration. Prerequisites include the client adhering to the general prohibition against including certain options (e.g., Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA) in the Option Request option, as these are handled separately in the message structure.

In DHCPv6, when a client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it MUST include an Option Request option (ORO) to specify which configuration options it wishes to receive from the server. For any top-level option (i.e., options not encapsulated within another option) that the client expects the server to include in its response, the corresponding option code MUST be listed in the ORO. If a top-level option is not explicitly requested in the ORO, the server will not include it in the reply, except for those options that are prohibited from appearing in the ORO (such as Client Identifier, Server Identifier, IA_NA, etc., as enumerated in the specification). This rule ensures that clients explicitly signal their desired options, allowing servers to optimize responses and avoid sending unsolicited data. Note that options encapsulated within container options generally SHOULD NOT be listed in the ORO, unless explicitly defined otherwise by a specific option's specification.

In DHCPv6, when a client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it MUST include an Option Request option (OPTION_ORO, code 6) to explicitly list the configuration or informational options it requires from the server. This rule applies during the initial configuration exchange and subsequent lease management phases to ensure the server only returns the requested top-level options, thereby optimizing message size and processing. The client must populate the option with the 2-octet codes of the desired options, excluding those prohibited (such as Client/Server Identifier, IA options, and other control options listed in the specification). The expected outcome is that the server, upon receiving a valid Option Request option, will include the requested options in its response, provided they are applicable and allowed. Prerequisites include the client knowing the specific option codes it needs, and the rule does not apply to other message types like Confirm, Decline, or Release.

When constructing an Option Request option in DHCPv6 messages (Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request), clients MAY only include top-level options in the requested-option-code list. This rule applies during message formation to inform the server which options the client wishes to receive. The expected outcome is that the server will return the requested top-level options in its response, while encapsulated options (those nested within container options like IA_NA, IA_TA, or IA_PD) SHOULD NOT be listed, unless explicitly defined by a specification that overrides this restriction (e.g., Prefix Exclude option). Prerequisites include excluding prohibited options (e.g., Client Identifier, Server Identifier) as mandated, and ensuring the option-len field correctly reflects twice the number of requested options.

The Option Request option (ORO) is used by a client in Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request messages to signal which configuration options it wishes the server to return. This rule explicitly prohibits the inclusion of specific option codes within the ORO's requested-option-code list. The prohibited options are those that are either mandatory for the message exchange (e.g., Client/Server Identifier, IA_NA, IA_TA, IA_PD), used for message handling and control rather than configuration data (e.g., Relay Message, Authentication, Status Code, Rapid Commit), or are inherently client-to-server signals themselves (e.g., Option Request, Elapsed Time, Reconfigure Accept). Including these in the ORO is redundant, incorrect, or would disrupt protocol semantics. The expected outcome is that a compliant client will never list these options in an ORO, and a server receiving an ORO containing them MUST ignore those specific entries. This rule applies whenever a client constructs an ORO, and it is a prerequisite that the implementer correctly identifies which options are considered "top-level" for this purpose, as encapsulated sub-options are also generally excluded.

When constructing an Option Request option (OPTION_ORO) in DHCPv6 messages (Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request), the general rule is that only top-level options may be listed, and options encapsulated within a container option (e.g., within an IA_NA or IA_PD) should not be included. However, future or specific option definitions MAY explicitly define exceptions to this restriction, allowing certain encapsulated options to be validly requested within the Option Request option. This exception mechanism is intended for cases where signaling support for a feature is required even when the corresponding option is normally encapsulated, such as the Prefix Exclude option defined in RFC 6603. Implementations MUST adhere to the base restriction unless processing an option whose specification explicitly overrides it.

The Authentication option (OPTION_AUTH, code 11) is used to carry authentication information that verifies the identity of the message source and ensures the integrity of DHCPv6 message contents. This option applies when authentication is required for DHCPv6 exchanges, such as during initial configuration or reconfiguration processes, and must be included in messages where authentication is negotiated or mandated. The expected outcome is that recipients can validate the message's authenticity and detect replay attacks, thereby preventing unauthorized access or tampering. Prerequisites include prior agreement on the authentication protocol, algorithm, and replay detection method (RDM) between the client and server, as specified by the protocol, algorithm, and RDM fields within the option. Note that the delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315 is obsolete, and the option format includes variable-length authentication information with a fixed header of 11 octets plus the authentication data length.

The Authentication option, which carries authentication information to verify the identity and contents of DHCPv6 messages, must be formatted as specified in Figure 22. This rule applies when implementing authentication for DHCPv6 exchanges as described in Section 20, and it supersedes the obsolete delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315. The expected outcome is a correctly structured option with fields including: option-code (OPTION_AUTH, 11), option-len (11 plus the length of the authentication information field), protocol (1 octet, specifying the authentication protocol), algorithm (1 octet, specifying the algorithm), RDM (1 octet, specifying the replay detection method), a fixed 64-bit replay detection field, and a variable-length authentication information field. Prerequisites include selecting registered protocol, algorithm, and RDM values from the IANA registry, and ensuring the option is included in messages where authentication is required.

When a DHCPv6 client sends a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it uses the Option Request option (ORO) to list the top-level options it wishes to receive from the server. This rule specifically governs the composition of that list. Options that are defined to be encapsulated within a container option (e.g., options carried inside an IA_NA, IA_PD, or other structured option as exemplified in RFC 7598) SHOULD NOT have their individual codes listed directly in the ORO. The expected outcome is that the client requests only the container option itself (e.g., OPTION_IA_NA), and the server, upon including that container in its response, will also include the appropriate encapsulated sub-options based on the protocol context. This rule applies because the server's logic for processing and including encapsulated options is typically tied to the presence and parameters of the parent container, not a direct request for the sub-option. A prerequisite is that the option in question is formally defined as being encapsulated within a known container. However, this is a "SHOULD NOT" recommendation, not an absolute prohibition; future option specifications MAY define explicit exceptions where requesting an encapsulated option via the ORO is a valid mechanism, such as for signaling feature support as noted for the Prefix Exclude option [RFC6603].

When a DHCPv6 client includes an Option Request option in a Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message, it is generally restricted to listing only top-level options to request from the server; encapsulated (container) options SHOULD NOT be listed. However, an exception to this restriction MAY be applied if a specific option definition explicitly permits it. In such cases, the Option Request option MAY be used to signal client support for a feature associated with an encapsulated option, even though the option itself is not directly requested at the top level. This applies when the protocol flow involves the client advertising its capability to the server, and the expected outcome is that the server becomes aware of the client's support for the feature, which may influence the server's subsequent response or option provisioning. A prerequisite is that the option's specification (e.g., RFC 6603 for the Prefix Exclude option) must define this exceptional usage.

The Authentication option, identified by the option-code OPTION_AUTH (11), is used to carry authentication information for verifying the identity and integrity of DHCPv6 messages. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any DHCPv6 message that includes authentication, as specified in Section 20 of the protocol. The expected outcome is that the option-code field is correctly set to the value 11 to designate this as an Authentication option. A prerequisite is that authentication is being employed; note that the delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315 is obsolete. The option must be formatted according to Figure 22, with the subsequent option-len field calculated as 11 plus the length of the variable-length authentication information field.
req_type: any DHCPv6 message that includes authentication

rule: The Authentication option (OPTION_AUTH, code 11) is used to carry authentication information that verifies the identity of the communicating parties and ensures the integrity of DHCPv6 messages. This rule applies when a DHCPv6 client or server includes the Authentication option in any message to enable authentication, as detailed in Section 20 of the specification, which covers authentication protocols, algorithms, and replay detection methods. The expected outcome is that recipients can validate the message's authenticity and detect replay attacks, provided they support the specified protocol, algorithm, and RDM (Replay Detection Method). Prerequisites include configuring both client and server with compatible authentication parameters and ensuring the delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315 is not used, as it has been obsoleted. The option format includes fixed fields for protocol, algorithm, RDM, and a 64-bit replay detection field, followed by a variable-length authentication information field, with the total length calculated as 11 octets plus the authentication information length.

When constructing an Authentication option (OPTION_AUTH, code 11) for DHCPv6 to authenticate message identity and contents, the `option-len` field must be calculated as the sum of 11 octets (which account for the fixed-length header fields: 1-octet protocol, 1-octet algorithm, 1-octet RDM, and the 8-octet replay detection field) plus the length in octets of the variable-length authentication information field. This rule applies during the creation of any DHCPv6 message that includes authentication, ensuring the option length accurately reflects the total size of the option for proper parsing. The expected outcome is a correctly formatted Authentication option where the receiver can derive the authentication information length by subtracting 11 from the `option-len` value. This calculation is a prerequisite only when the Authentication option is included, following the updated protocols specified in this document (the delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315 is obsolete).

The RDM (Replay Detection Method) field within the Authentication option specifies the method used to protect against message replay attacks by defining how the 64-bit replay detection field is interpreted and validated. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any DHCPv6 message that includes an Authentication option (option-code 11) to ensure secure message exchange. The expected outcome is that both the client and server consistently use the RDM value to generate and verify the replay detection information, thereby preventing the acceptance of duplicated or out-of-sequence messages. This field is mandatory within the Authentication option and must be set to a value registered with IANA, with its interpretation directly tied to the chosen authentication protocol and algorithm.
req_type: any DHCPv6 message that includes an Authentication option

rule: The protocol field within the Authentication option (option-code 11) is a mandatory 1-octet unsigned integer that specifies the authentication protocol used to authenticate the identity and contents of the DHCPv6 message. This rule applies when constructing or parsing an Authentication option, which is included in DHCPv6 messages as defined in Section 20. The field must be set to a value registered with IANA for authentication namespaces, and it determines the interpretation of the subsequent algorithm, RDM, and authentication information fields. The expected outcome is that both client and server correctly identify and process the specified authentication protocol, enabling successful message validation. A prerequisite is that the Authentication option is being used, and the delayed authentication protocol from RFC3315 is not applicable as it has been obsoleted.

When constructing or parsing an Authentication option (OPTION_AUTH, code 11) in a DHCPv6 message, the length of the variable-length `authentication information` field must be calculated as the value of the `option-len` field minus 11 octets. This rule applies after the fixed-length header fields (protocol, algorithm, RDM, and the 8-octet replay detection field) have been accounted for, ensuring the total option length matches the specified `option-len`. The expected outcome is a correctly formatted Authentication option where the authentication data precisely fills the remaining length indicated by the option header. This calculation is a prerequisite for any message authentication and must be performed whenever the Authentication option is present.

The authentication information field within the Authentication option (OPTION_AUTH) must contain the authentication data (e.g., a MAC or hash) as defined by the specific authentication protocol (e.g., the reconfigured key protocol) and cryptographic algorithm indicated in the option's protocol and algorithm fields. This rule applies when constructing or validating any DHCPv6 message that includes an Authentication option for integrity and identity verification. The expected outcome is that the field's content and length correctly correspond to the chosen protocol-algorithm pair, enabling the recipient to authenticate the message. Prerequisites include selecting a valid, registered protocol and algorithm (as maintained by IANA) and calculating the authentication data using the specified method and the appropriate key material. The field length is variable and is determined by the formula: (option-len value - 11) octets.
req_type: any DHCPv6 message that includes an Authentication option

rule: The IA_TA-options field within the Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option is variable in length. This length is derived by subtracting 4 octets from the value specified in the option-len field of the IA_TA option header. This rule applies when constructing or parsing an IA_TA option, as the option-len field encodes the total length of the entire IA_TA option (including the fixed 4-octet IAID field). Therefore, the IA_TA-options field, which contains sub-options like IA Address options and Status Code options specific to this IA_TA, occupies the remaining portion of the option. Correct adherence ensures accurate parsing of the encapsulated options and proper message formatting. A prerequisite is that the option-len value must be at least 4 to account for the mandatory IAID field.

When constructing an Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option in a DHCPv6 message, the value of the `option-len` field must be calculated as the sum of 4 (the fixed-length portion containing the `option-code` and `IAID`) and the total length in octets of the variable-length `IA_TA-options` field that follows. This rule applies whenever a client or server generates an IA_TA option, such as during address assignment (Solicit/Advertise/Request/Reply), renewal (Renew/Rebind), or release. The expected outcome is a correctly formatted option where the length field accurately reflects the total size of the option data, enabling the recipient to parse the `IA_TA-options` field correctly. A prerequisite is that the `IA_TA-options` field itself must be properly structured, containing encapsulated options like IA Address options for temporary addresses or a Status Code option.

The OPTION_IA_TA (option-code 4) identifies the Identity Association for Temporary Addresses option, which is used exclusively to manage sets of temporary IPv6 addresses for a client, as defined in RFC 4941. This option applies when a client requests, renews, or rebinds temporary addresses, and it must only appear within the options area of a DHCPv6 message such as Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Reply. The expected outcome is the encapsulation of a unique IA_TA, identified by a distinct IAID, along with its associated temporary address parameters and IA Address options within the IA_TA-options field. Prerequisites include that the IAID must be unique among all IA_TAs for the client and must not overlap with IAIDs used for IA_NA or IA_PD options. The option length is calculated as 4 octets (for the IAID) plus the length of the IA_TA-options field, and the option does not include T1 or T2 timers; address lifetimes are managed individually per address.

When a DHCPv6 server assigns temporary addresses to a client using an Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option, all IA Address options (defined in Section 21.6) that specify the actual IPv6 addresses associated with that IA_TA must be encapsulated within the IA_TA-options field. This rule applies during the address assignment or renewal process when the server constructs a Reply, Renew-Reply, or Rebind-Reply message containing an IA_TA. The expected outcome is that the client receives a coherent IA_TA structure where all related address information is contained within the IA_TA-options field, enabling proper processing and management of temporary addresses. A prerequisite is that the IA_TA option must be included in the message, and the server must have allocated at least one temporary address for the IA_TA identified by its unique IAID.

The IA_TA-options field is a variable-length field within the Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option, which is used to encapsulate all DHCPv6 options that are specifically associated with that IA_TA instance. This rule applies whenever an IA_TA option is constructed in a DHCPv6 message, such as in Solicit, Advertise, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Reply messages. The expected outcome is that the field contains a structured set of sub-options, which must include at least one IA Address option (Section 21.6) carrying the temporary IPv6 addresses assigned to the IA_TA, and may optionally include a Status Code option (Section 21.13) to indicate the success or failure of operations related to this IA_TA. A prerequisite is that the IA_TA option itself must be present with a valid, unique IAID, and the length of the IA_TA-options field must be exactly 4 octets less than the value specified in the option's overall length (option-len) field.

The IA_TA-options field is a variable-length field within the Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option, used to encapsulate all DHCPv6 options that pertain specifically to the IA_TA identified by its unique IAID. This rule applies when constructing or parsing an IA_TA option in any DHCPv6 message (e.g., Solicit, Advertise, Request, Reply, Renew, Rebind). The expected outcome is that any option directly related to the addresses or status of this particular IA_TA, such as IA Address options carrying the temporary IPv6 addresses themselves or a Status Code option indicating the result of an operation on this IA_TA, must be placed within this encapsulated field. A prerequisite is that the IA_TA option must be present, and the length of the IA_TA-options field must be exactly the value of the option-len field minus 4 octets (accounting for the fixed-length IAID). This encapsulation ensures that options are correctly associated with their specific IA_TA, especially when multiple IA_TAs are present in a single message.

The IA_TA-options field is a variable-length field within the Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) option, which is used to encapsulate all DHCPv6 options that are specifically associated with that IA_TA instance. This rule applies whenever an IA_TA option is constructed in a DHCPv6 message, such as in Solicit, Advertise, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Reply messages. The expected outcome is that the field contains a structured set of sub-options, which must include at least one IA Address option (Section 21.6) carrying the temporary IPv6 addresses assigned to the IA_TA, and may optionally include a Status Code option (Section 21.13) to indicate the success or failure of operations related to this IA_TA. A prerequisite is that the IA_TA option itself must be present with a valid, unique IAID, and the length of the IA_TA-options field must be exactly 4 octets less than the value specified in the option's overall length (option-len) field.

The IA_TA option, which encapsulates a set of temporary addresses and their associated parameters for a client, must be placed exclusively within the options area of a DHCP message. This rule applies to all DHCP message types (e.g., Solicit, Advertise, Request, Renew, Rebind) where IA_TA options are used. Compliance ensures proper message parsing and processing by DHCP agents, as the options area is the designated location for such configuration data. The rule is unconditional; there are no prerequisites other than the use of an IA_TA option itself. A DHCP message may contain multiple IA_TA options, each with a unique IAID, but all must reside in the options area.

Within a single DHCPv6 message, a client or server may include multiple Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA) options to manage distinct sets of temporary addresses. This rule applies when a client is requesting new temporary addresses, renewing existing ones, or when a server is responding with assigned addresses. Each IA_TA option present in the message must have a unique Identity Association Identifier (IAID) to unambiguously distinguish the separate sets of temporary addresses. The IAID uniqueness is scoped specifically to IA_TA options; it is independent of the IAID number space used for Identity Association for Non-temporary Addresses (IA_NA) or Identity Association for Prefix Delegation (IA_PD) options. This structure allows a client to manage multiple independent groups of temporary addresses with different lifetimes or for different purposes within the same protocol exchange.

When a DHCPv6 server processes a client's request involving an Identity Association for Temporary Addresses (IA_TA), the outcome of that operation (e.g., success, failure, or specific error conditions) must be communicated to the client through a Status Code option placed within the IA_TA-options field of the IA_TA option. This rule applies during any message exchange where an IA_TA is used, such as in Solicit, Request, Renew, Rebind, or Reply messages, whenever the server needs to indicate the status of address assignment, renewal, or release operations specific to that IA_TA. The expected outcome is that the client can unambiguously determine the success or failure of the request for its temporary addresses by examining the Status Code within the IA_TA-options. A prerequisite for this rule is that an IA_TA option is present in the DHCP message, and the server is generating a response that requires status indication for operations on that specific IA_TA.

The User Class option (OPTION_USER_CLASS, code 15) is used by a DHCPv6 client to identify its user or application category, enabling the server to provide tailored configuration (e.g., department-specific settings like printer assignments). This option applies when a client includes it in a DHCPv6 message to convey its class membership. The format consists of the OPTION_USER_CLASS code, an option-len field specifying the total length of the following user-class-data, and one or more user-class-data instances, each containing a 2-octet user-class-len and opaque-data. The client must have configurable user class information, and the server must interpret only recognized classes, optionally echoing them back in its response. The expected outcome is that the server selects appropriate configuration parameters based on the interpreted classes, ignoring any unrecognized ones.

During the address renewal phase of the DHCPv6 stateful address assignment process, a client that has been assigned temporary addresses (as defined in RFC 4941) via an IA_TA option MAY request an extension of the valid lifetime for those specific addresses. This is performed by including the existing IA_TA option, containing the addresses to be extended, within a Renew or Rebind message sent to the server. The primary expected outcome is to allow established connections (e.g., long-lived TCP sessions) using a temporary address to continue beyond the original valid lifetime, though the address will eventually become deprecated as only the valid lifetime is extended. This rule applies only when the client is in the RENEWING or REBINDING state for its temporary addresses, and the prerequisite is that the client possesses a currently valid IA_TA with associated addresses whose lifetimes have not yet fully expired. The server, upon receiving this request, will process it according to its policy, potentially granting the extension and returning updated lifetimes in its reply.

The option-len field specifies the total length in octets of the user-class-data field within the User Class option. This length must accurately reflect the combined size of all user-class-data instances included, where each instance consists of a 2-octet user-class-len field followed by its corresponding opaque-data. The rule applies when a DHCPv6 client includes the User Class option in a message to identify its user categories, and the server uses this length to correctly parse the option data for selecting appropriate configuration parameters. The expected outcome is that the server can interpret the user classes based on this length, enabling targeted configuration (e.g., assigning department-specific resources). Prerequisites include that the user-class-data must contain at least one instance and be configurable on the client, and the option-len must be consistent with the sum of all user-class-len values plus their overhead.

The Interface-Id option's interface-id field contains an opaque, variable-length identifier generated by a relay agent. The exact length of this interface-id field in octets is explicitly defined by the value in the option-len field of the option header. This length specification is mandatory for parsing the option correctly in Relay-forward and Relay-reply messages, ensuring the relay agent can accurately identify the intended interface when relaying the DHCPv6 message to the client. The server must treat this value as an opaque byte sequence without internal parsing, using it solely for policy matching based on its exact length and content.

The User Class option (option-code 15) is used by a DHCPv6 client to identify the user or application categories it represents, enabling the server to provide tailored configuration. This option applies when a client includes it in a Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind message to convey its class membership. The expected outcome is that the server interprets the provided user classes according to its own configuration and selects appropriate, class-specific parameters (e.g., printer settings for different departments) for inclusion in its response. A prerequisite is that the user class data must be configurable on the client and formatted as one or more instances of user-class-data, each with a 2-octet length field and opaque data. The server may ignore unrecognized classes and can optionally echo back the successfully interpreted classes in its reply to inform the client.
req_fields: user-class-data (with 2-octet length field and opaque data)

rule: The User Class option (option-code 15) allows a DHCPv6 client to declare its membership in one or more user categories, which the server uses to provide tailored configuration. The `user-class-data` field MUST contain one or more opaque data instances, each prefixed by a 2-octet `user-class-len` field (in network byte order) specifying the length of the subsequent opaque data. This option is included in client messages (e.g., Solicit, Request) when the client requires class-specific configuration; the server then interprets only those user classes it recognizes from its configuration to select appropriate parameters (e.g., different printer settings for accounting vs. marketing departments). The server MAY echo back recognized classes in its reply to inform the client. Prerequisites: The user class data MUST be configurable on the client, and the server must be configured to map specific user class values to corresponding configuration actions.

When a relay agent receives a Relay-reply message containing an Interface-Id option, it must use the opaque interface identifier value within that option to determine the specific local interface through which to forward the message to the client. This rule applies during the downstream relay phase, after the server has processed the Relay-forward message and returned a Relay-reply.
